body_txt: The Stone Wall is a member of the Competitive Balance who is characterized by the distinctive traits of great defense mixed with terrible offense. An old saying teaches that the best defense is a good offense, but the Stone Wall believes defense by itself is enough. They can take it, but they can't dish it out. Their biggest strength is that they can take anything their opponents can throw at them and stay standing, while their biggest weakness is that they can barely scratch their opponents in return. The Stone Wall's offense is nothing to write home about, if it even technically exists . But they're tough. Really, really tough . They can shrug off blows that would kill a Fragile Speedster thrice over. And if anything can put them down, odds are they're quick enough on the recovery to get right back up for round two. They're rarely the protagonist, since the audience wants a main character that kicks ass by themselves. Such a character is frequently referred to as a "tank" in gamer lingo, named after the weapon of war that is designed to be a mobile fortress and incredibly hard to bring down . A Stone Wall's strategy is often known as "turtling" in the same parlanche, named for a turtle's tendency to retreat into its hard-to-break shell as a response to a threat. In warfare, the strategy is a battle of attrition to see who tires out or makes a mistake first. In sports, a team of Stone Walls often favour a waiting game where they get an early lead over the other team, then spend the rest of the match running out the clock by doing everything they can to counter the other side's strategies and slow the game down to a crawl. If the Wall's defense is something they physically construct and build, they can win a fight by slowly expanding outward until they leave the enemy without a foot to stand on. Stone Walls often have defensive buffs and minor healing magic in order to allow them to survive longer. The Paladin is often portrayed as this trope mixed with the White Mage in games that don't have a Karma Meter . In rare cases, they may also have access to Fixed-Damage Attacks , which by their very nature ignore stats, giving them at least a little access to actual offensive power. However, more offensively oriented characters will still always have a higher damage output with regular attacks. Although in very rare cases, typically at a high level, they'll gain access to an attack that scales off their Defense rather than their Attack (or whatever stat the Stone Wall's resilience comes from), allowing them to deal some serious damage at last. Alternatively, Stone Walls can try the opposite tactic, berserking: throwing themselves at the enemy without a thought for defense. Relying on their inherent toughness to keep them alive, Stone Walls can use attacks that would be suicidal for a weaker character and status-changing moves to make up for their dismal attack power. This strategy is especially common for Stone Walls whose toughness is completely automatic , rather than something they need to work at. A Stone Wall may also be equipped with stat manipulating moves, allowing it to spend several turns 'setting up' by increasing its defense even further or raising its attack and speed , before going on the offensive. In team settings, a Stone Wall often takes care of " tanking " duties, interposing himself between the enemy and an ally, typically a Glass Cannon that can take care of dealing damage while the Stone Wall takes care of defense. By keeping the enemy occupied, he allows allies with greater attack strength but poorer defense to kill the enemy without getting killed. Characters who do this are called "Meat Shields" or "Party Tanks". They often have moves designed to force attention to themselves. Sometimes called "Control Tanks". Finally, a character who relies on drawing attention to themselves and then dodging the attacks instead of enduring them is known as a "Dodge Tank", though such a character archetype is relatively uncommon compared to the other types. This build has a number of flaws that can be exploited. Fixed and percent damage attacks , along with armor piercing attacks , ignore their thick defenses. Defense-reduction debuffs may be somewhat effective in exposing them to peril, but Damage Over Time debuffs are invariably fatal unless cured. And if the break meter is directly tied to your defensive ability, expect anything with the ability to damage it to do so with extreme prejudice. An Instakill Mook will also be able to one-shot it as if its defenses aren't even there. In lower-tier Stone Walls, one of the defenses will be much lower than the rest, to keep them balanced: popular weak points include a weakness to non-physical attacks , vulnerability to a certain weapon or element , or a far squishier interior . An interesting variant comes in Racing Games : like Mighty Glaciers in this genre, these vehicles are often heavy and good to push opponents, but unlike them, they don't focus the rest of their power on top speed to increase their destructive potential: instead, they focus more on " lightweight " stats like acceleration, off-road or traction. They can have a good top speed, but they will focus on the stability of the vehicle on land and its ability to recover despite its heavyweight. Red Oni, Blue Oni can be applied to this build in two ways: on one hand, the Blue relies upon patience and endurance over relentless assault, and it is not unheard of for a Stone Wall to simply outlast their opposition; on the other hand, the Red is a weak attacker, but their resistance and perseverance will progressively (albeit unintentionally) exhaust their enemy. For both, when time is especially crucial, the Stone Wall can be a nightmare to defeat, since they would most likely win by a time-out if their opponent fails to land a significant blow. This trope partly takes its name from a real-life example: Confederate General Thomas "Stonewall" Jackson won the Battle of Bull Run due to his strategy to not retreat from his line, no matter how bad things went for him. And for a while, things went pretty bad. General Jackson himself is not an example of this trope despite the nickname, as outside of that particular battle, he was most noted for his offensive campaigns. Contrast Glass Cannon , which is the Opposite Trope with strong offense and weak defense, and Lightning Bruiser , which is tough and fast without sacrificing strength. Also contrast Mighty Glacier , who is strong and tough but lacks speed; and Fragile Speedster , who has poor defense, but is fast. Not an unusual trait of the Weak, but Skilled , Gentle Giant , Cowardly Lion , or Iron Butt-Monkey . Padded Sumo Gameplay is what happens when everyone in a game is a Stone Wall. Beware the Death of a Thousand Cuts , which occurs when the Stone Wall is incapable of reprisal and the enemy is sufficiently determined .
